title: Classic Upholstered Chair
summary: A simple accent chair with a 2x4 back-leg frame, an angled seat back, and a padded seat you staple and upholster yourself.
description:
Getting both back legs to match is the entire skill in this build, and the source's own advice is worth taking literally: practice the leg cut on a scrap piece of 2x4 before committing to the real stock. Cut as much as possible with a circular saw, finishing only the interior curves with a jigsaw, then lay the two finished legs together and check that the top, bottom, and front edges all line up — those are the surfaces that show and bear load, so they're the ones worth double-checking; the rest matters far less.

The upholstery is a straightforward staple-and-wrap job, not sewing: spray-glue the foam to the plywood seat top, lay fabric face-down with the foam-side seat on top, then staple the fabric to the underside, tucking corners neatly as you go. A chair like this is a genuinely inexpensive build — the source's own version came in under $20 in materials.

tools.note: Finishes the interior curves on the back legs.

cutList.note: Practice on scrap first; front cut inset 1/4" for rounded 2x4 edges.
Both ends cut at 45°, not parallel to each other.
Longest point measurement, one end cut at 8° off square.

steps.title: Cut the back legs
steps.body: Mark and cut the back legs, insetting the front cut (where the leg connects to the side aprons) by 1/4" to account for the rounded edges on 2x4 stock. Make as many cuts as possible with a circular saw, finishing the interior cuts on the back with a jigsaw. Practice the cut on a scrap piece of 2x4 first. Once cut, lay both legs together and check that the top, bottom, and front sides all match — the other edges matter less. Sand the cuts smooth.

steps.title: Attach the side aprons
steps.body: Attach the side aprons to the back legs, flush to the bottom of the front cuts, checking that the two leg pieces still line up — a mismatch here means a crooked chair.

steps.title: Attach the front legs
steps.body: Attach the front legs to the side aprons, tops flush, checking the chair parts still line up.

steps.title: Attach the front and back aprons
steps.body: Attach the front and back aprons, predrilling carefully. Pocket hole screws and glue work well here, tops flush.

steps.title: Attach the seat back top
steps.body: Attach the seat back top piece.

steps.title: Attach the seat back
steps.body: Attach the seat back boards, using pocket holes from the back or predrilled screws from the legs.

steps.title: Attach the base supports
steps.body: Attach the base side supports — these add considerable strength to the chair.

steps.title: Attach the base center support
steps.body: Attach the base center support to the sides. Add the corner braces for extra strength, with glue.

steps.title: Upholster the seat
steps.body: Spray-glue the foam cushion to the plywood seat top. Lay the fabric face-down on a table with the seat top on top of it, foam side down. Staple the fabric carefully to the underside of the seat, tucking corners neatly. Attach the finished seat to the cross supports with 2" screws or pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every exposed wood surface and paint or stain as desired.

images.alt: Classic upholstered accent chair with 2x4 back legs
